body.u: At 69 I have often looked back over my career. I left school at 18 and went on to university. I started my career at 21 with a degree. My career was geology. One of the reasons why I chose geology was that I found it very interesting and it also included lots of travel. The pay wasn’t bad too!

At school, especially the term before my ‘O’ levels it was very bored, but during this time I went to see the careers officedoffice. Our school, Sheppey Comprehensive, was very large and I didn’t like going to school, because it was so big. When I went into the sixth forform to study ‘A’ levels things seemed to change. I enjoyed going to school. The sixth form was a pleasant area, carpeted etc. I was a little sorry to leave it to go to university.
A university student was a great life, with many opportunities to do what I liked. Holidays were also great, and the friends were always good for a laugh.

When I was 21 I left university with my geology degree and I got a job with the civil service. I was soon travelling all round the world studying these land formation and those land formations. Some of the surveying was also in the seas as well. And I found my career thoroughly enjoyable. I was very sorry to retire four years ago.
At the age of forty I settled down to study the rocks of my own local environment until I retired.
After first reading about geology at school, when I was about 15, I became hooked on the subject. It seemed to fascinate me but a year later I had gone off it, but I still wanted to be a geologist.

It was nearly hopeless when I tried the careers centre. They could give me some information of the exams I would need and on further education (i.e. university) but they could not really give me and leaflets on the actual subject of geology. The only real information I obtained was from book from the library or from a few science lessons from school. To become a geologist, I had to put in a lot of work for myself. Once I had made it though I was laughing. The job with the civil service was great. Most of it was a holiday not a job. I was often being sent away to the far reaches of the earth, in, sometimes, the remotest places. I had to work in the extrimesextreme of tempreturetemperatures and in almost every country throughout the world.

It wasn’t all plain sailing though sometimes I had to work under the great depths of the ocean, or the high mountains. I even had to work near volcanoes, even the active ones. Where there was an earthquake disaster I was sent there, especially those where a seismograph station wasn’t very near.
Other times I had paper work to at seismograph station. I had to take sesmic readings of earth tremours plot them on graphs and other various paper work, but this was only fractional.

On the bright side of my career though, I often visited exotic places, which people only dream about. I also had long holidays and the pay was good, with an increase in yearly salaries. With this money I was able to sometimes, really “live it up”. The great attraction though was the travel, and my interest in the land.

During my spare time, I was often on holidays, either camping/hiking, or on a cycle tour. These kind of holidays were always the best for me. I always seemed to enjoy the most. I found that these holidays always added to the good things in life.
At 28 I got married to a French girl and she has also travelled with me, when I was at work, and also on holidays.

Looking back to the struggle of becoming a geologist, I think that if I had not made it I would have tried to become a warden of a national park or perhaps something like that e.g. a game keeper, forrestryforestry warden, so long as it was something I could enjoy a great deal.

Even now, in my large farmhouse (but there isn’t a farm no more) on one of the hymalayanHimalayan mountains. I look back and thing to myself, what a fantastic life I’ve leadhad, and that I wish I hadn’t retired from geology. I still study the various rock formations of the HymalayanHimalayan range, here near Tibet.
